title: Data Management in R
text:
Index to my data management notes in Quarto.
Future topics to cover:

date handling,
pivoting,
handle strings,
variable labels.

title: 2  Data being used
text:
2.1 Packages

title: 3  Numeric variables
text:
3.1 Mean of single variables
df <- df %>% mutate(libauth_av = mean(libauth, na.rm = T), 
                    leftrigh_av = mean(leftrigh, na.rm = T))

title: 3  Numeric variables
section: 3.2 Mean of multiple variables at once (each mean is separate)
text:
3.2 Mean of multiple variables at once (each mean is separate)

3.2.1 A purrr-style formula

df <- df %>% mutate(
  across(
    c(welfare2:libauth), 
    ~mean(.x, na.rm = T), 
    .names = "{.col}_mean")
  )

df %>% count(libauth_mean)

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  libauth_mean     n
         <dbl> <int>
1         2.80  3224

df %>% count(leftrigh_mean)

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  leftrigh_mean     n
          <dbl> <int>
1          2.01  3224

df %>% count(welfare2_mean)

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  welfare2_mean     n
          <dbl> <int>
1          2.16  3224

title: 3  Numeric variables
text:
3.3 Calculate multiple statistics per variable

3.3.1 Requires a named list of functions
Adds a column with the mean of welfare2 and the standard deviation.

df %>% select(welfare2) %>% 
  mutate(
    across(
      all_of("welfare2"), 
      # First part is name for stat column, second is the statistic function
      list(var_mean = mean, sd = sd), 
      .names = "{.col}_{.fn}")
    )

title: 3  Numeric variables
text:
3.4 Selecting variables to edit

3.4.1 Selecting variables based on type
Can select variables to be edited based on variable type as well.

df %>% select(Sserial, WtFactor, welfare2, libauth) %>%
  mutate(
    across(
      where(is.double) & !c(Sserial),
      # Otherwise, name comes out as WtFactor_1 
      list(rounded = round), 
      .names = "{.col}_{.fn}"
      )
    ) %>% 
  glimpse

4.2.1 Refining ntile

# Function to add labels to ntile breaks
ntile_lab <- function(data, var, breaks = 5) { 
  
  var_to_use <- data %>% pull(var)
  
  vec <- ntile(zap_missing(var_to_use), n = breaks)
  
  breaks <- map(na.omit(unique(vec)),
                ~paste0(
                  min(zap_missing(var_to_use)[vec == .x],na.rm = T), " - ",
                  max(zap_missing(var_to_use)[vec == .x],na.rm = T)
                )
  )
  vec_unique <-  as.vector(na.omit(unique(vec)))
  names(vec_unique) <-  breaks
  vec_unique
  
  result <- vec %>% set_value_labels(.labels = vec_unique)
  
  return(result)
  
  }

# convenience function to run count() for all of a vector of variables
count_df <- function(df, vars) {
  map(vars, 
      ~df %>%
        mutate_at(.x, ~as_factor(.x, levels = "both")) %>%
        count(across(all_of(.x))) %>% 
        mutate(var = .x) %>% 
        rename(cat = .x) %>% 
        relocate(var)
      ) %>%
    bind_rows
}

check_recode <- function(df, vars, suffix) {
  for (var in vars) {

    cat("\n===", var, "===========================\n")
    result <- count(df, .data[[var]], .data[[new_var]])
    print(result, n = nrow(result))
    cat("\n")
  }
}

title: 9  tidy-select
text:
9.3 Create some test data

# Function to generate labelled_spss vectors with missing values defined
generate_var <- function(n = 1000) {
  labelled_spss(
    sample(
      c(1:5, 8, 9, NA),
      size = n,
      replace = TRUE,
      prob = c(0.15, 0.2, 0.25, 0.2, 0.1, 0.05, 0.03, 0.02)
    ),
    labels = c(
      "Strongly agree" = 1,
      "Agree" = 2,
      "Neither agree nor disagree" = 3,
      "Disagree" = 4,
      "Strongly disagree" = 5,
      "Don't know" = 8,
      "Refused" = 9
    ),
    na_values = c(8, 9) # <- user-defined missing values
  )
}

# Create dummy data set
set.seed(123)
dummy_data <- tibble(
  q1 = generate_var(),
  q2 = generate_var(),
  q3 = generate_var(),
  q4 = generate_var(),
  q5 = generate_var()
)

title: 9  tidy-select
text:
9.4 Combining factor and case_when
Using across to apply edits across multiple variables…

# Using case_when() and factor()
dummy_data <- 
  dummy_data %>% 
  mutate(across(everything(), 
                ~factor(
                  case_when(.x <= 2 ~ 1, 
                           .x == 3 ~ 2, 
                           .x >= 4 & .x < 8 ~ 3), 
                  levels = c(1,2,3), 
                  labels = c("Agree","Neither","Disagree")),
                .names = "{.col}_2"
                )
         ) 

dummy_data %>% count(q1, q1_2)

  q1                              q1_2         n

title: 9  tidy-select
text:
9.5 Recoding variables while labelled()
This is nice as it’s quick to type and combines the labels from the old ones into the new ones.

# Using recode from labelled()
dummy_data <- 
  dummy_data %>% 
  mutate(across(!contains("_2"), 
                ~recode(user_na_to_na(.x),
                  `1` = 1L, 
                  `2` = 1L, 
                  `3` = 2L, 
                  `4` = 3L,
                  `5` = 3L, 
                  .combine_value_labels = T
                  ),
                .names = "{.col}_2"
  ))
   

dummy_data %>% count(q1, q1_2) %>% mutate_if(is.labelled, as_factor, levels = "both")

# A tibble: 8 × 3
  q1                             q1_2                                 n
  <fct>                          <fct>                            <int>
1 [1] Strongly agree             [1] Strongly agree / Agree         150

4 [4] Disagree                   [3] Disagree / Strongly disagree   210
5 [5] Strongly disagree          [3] Disagree / Strongly disagree   106
6 [8] Don't know                 <NA>                                39
7 [9] Refused                    <NA>                                34
8 <NA>                           <NA>                                19




# using fct_recode from forcats...
dummy_data <- 
  dummy_data %>% 
    mutate(across(!contains("_2"), 
                  ~fct_recode(to_factor(.x), 
         "Agree" = "Strongly agree", 
         "Agree" = "Agree", 
         "Disagree" = "Disagree", 
         "Disagree" = "Strongly disagree"
                    ), 
         .names = "{.col}_2")
         )

title: 10  labelled
text:
10.10 labelled version of recode
This is an extension to the method from dplyr for labelled numeric data.
Although recode is called below with dplyr, this functionality is provided by labelled.

# Recoding with the labelled package
x <- labelled(1:3, c(yes = 1, no = 2))
x

<labelled<integer>[3]>
[1] 1 2 3

Labels:
 value label
     1   yes
     2    no

# Code below sets number 3 to be 2, and uses it's label.
dplyr::recode(x,`3` = 2L, .combine_value_labels = T)

<labelled<integer>[3]>
[1] 1 2 2

Labels:
 value label
     1   yes
     2    no


Where combined values have a label, these are combined…

x <- labelled(1:3, c(yes = 1, no = 2, maybe = 3))
x

<labelled<integer>[3]>
[1] 1 2 3

Labels:
 value label
     1   yes
     2    no
     3 maybe

dplyr::recode(x,`3` = 2L, .combine_value_labels = T)

<labelled<integer>[3]>
[1] 1 2 2

Labels:
 value      label
     1        yes
     2 no / maybe


A slightly realistic example

# Grouping agree / disagree style scale...
x <- labelled(
  1:4,
  c(
    "strongly agree" = 1,
    "agree" = 2,
    "disagree" = 3,
    "strongly disagree" = 4
  )
)

x

<labelled<integer>[4]>
[1] 1 2 3 4

Labels:
 value             label
     1    strongly agree
     2             agree
     3          disagree
     4 strongly disagree


Then, merge groups labels, .sep allows user defined break.

dplyr::recode(
  x,
  `1` = 1L,
  `2` = 1L,
  `3` = 2L,
  `4` = 2L,
  .combine_value_labels = TRUE, 
  .sep = " or "
)

<labelled<integer>[4]>
[1] 1 1 2 2

Labels:
 value                         label
     1       strongly agree or agree
     2 disagree or strongly disagree


Here recode drops value labels, so values are combined but labels dropped. Useful where combining labels is not helpful.

dplyr::recode(
  x,
  `1` = 1L,
  `2` = 1L,
  `3` = 2L,
  `4` = 2L,
  .keep_value_labels = F,
)

[1] 1 1 2 2



10.10.1 tidyr style labelled recode
Create a test data frame for tidyr style recoding

test_df <- test_df %>% set_value_labels(v1 = c(Yes = 1, No = 2, Maybe = 3))
test_df

  v1 v2 v3
1  1 NA  3
2  2 NA  2
3  3  1  1


Application within mutate()

test_df$v1

<labelled<integer>[3]>
[1] 1 2 3

Labels:
 value label
     1   Yes
     2    No
     3 Maybe

test_df <- test_df %>% 
  mutate(v1a = recode(v1, 
                      `1` = 1L, 
                      `2` = 1L, 
                      .combine_value_labels = TRUE)
         )

test_df$v1a

<labelled<integer>[3]>
[1] 1 1 3

Labels:
 value    label
     1 Yes / No
     3    Maybe

test_df %>% count(as_factor(v1), as_factor(v1a)) # Review result

  as_factor(v1) as_factor(v1a) n
1           Yes       Yes / No 1
2            No       Yes / No 1
3         Maybe          Maybe 1

title: 10  labelled
text:
10.11 Convert labelled data to factors
Application of to_factor() - convert labelled data to factors

v <- labelled(c(1, 2, 2, 2, 3, 9, 1, 3, 2, NA), c(yes = 1, no = 3, maybe = 2))
v

<labelled<double>[10]>
 [1]  1  2  2  2  3  9  1  3  2 NA

Labels:
 value label
     1   yes
     3    no
     2 maybe


The labelled() to_factor() function seems similar to as_factor()

v %>% to_factor() %>% levels()

[1] "yes"   "maybe" "no"    "9"    

v %>% as_factor() %>% levels()

[1] "yes"   "maybe" "no"    "9"    


Options…

v

<labelled<double>[10]>
 [1]  1  2  2  2  3  9  1  3  2 NA

Labels:
 value label
     1   yes
     3    no
     2 maybe

to_factor(v) # labels as levels

 [1] yes   maybe maybe maybe no    9     yes   no    maybe <NA> 
Levels: yes maybe no 9

to_factor(v, levels = "v") # values not labels

 [1] 1    2    2    2    3    9    1    3    2    <NA>
Levels: 1 2 3 9

to_factor(v, levels ="p") # both

 [1] [1] yes   [2] maybe [2] maybe [2] maybe [3] no    [9] 9     [1] yes  
 [8] [3] no    [2] maybe <NA>     
Levels: [1] yes [2] maybe [3] no [9] 9



10.11.1 Missing data

to_factor(v, nolabel_to_na = T) # unalabelled values to NA

 [1] yes   maybe maybe maybe no    <NA>  yes   no    maybe <NA> 
Levels: yes no maybe




10.11.2 Factor order

to_factor(v, sort_levels = "n") %>% levels() # Order is the order labels were defined in

[1] "yes"   "no"    "maybe" "9"    

to_factor(v, sort_levels = "v") %>% levels() # Order by values 

[1] "yes"   "maybe" "no"    "9"    

to_factor(v, sort_levels = "l") %>% levels() # Order by labels 

[1] "9"     "maybe" "no"    "yes"

10.12 Convert factors to labelled data
Back to labelled (perhaps for easy edits?).
Note values now 1:4, not 1, 2, 3 and 9.
1:4 is what as.numeric would return when applied to this factor.

f <- to_factor(v)
to_labelled(f) 

<labelled<double>[10]>
 [1]  1  2  2  2  3  4  1  3  2 NA

Labels:
 value label
     1   yes
     2 maybe
     3    no
     4     9

11.2 coalesce
From help:
Given a set of vectors, coalesce() finds the first non-missing value at each position.
It’s inspired by the SQL COALESCE function which does the same thing for SQL NULLs.

coalesce_data <- 
  data_frame(
  v1 = sample(c(1:10, NA), 100, replace = T), 
  v2 = sample(c(1:10, NA), 100, replace = T),
  v3 = sample(c(1:10, NA), 100, replace = T)
)
